section.title: The Cycle of Apostasy
section.sectionRationale: Here the narrator steps back and shows the reader the machinery of the whole book. A new generation arises that does not know the LORD, and the pattern begins to turn: the people do evil and serve the Baals, the LORD hands them over to plunderers, they groan, He raises up a judge to save them, and when the judge dies they turn back worse than before. This programmatic summary explains everything that follows and frames the nations left in the land as a test of Israel's heart.
section.thesis: When a generation forgets what the LORD has done, worship collapses into idolatry and the nation spirals through sin, servitude, supplication, salvation, and silence, each turn sinking lower; yet the recurring mercy of a God who is moved by His people's groaning and keeps raising up deliverers points beyond every failing judge to a Savior who will not die and leave His people to relapse.
section.frameNote: Three movements: a generation arises that did not know the LORD, verses 6 to 10; the cycle of apostasy and deliverance laid out in full, verses 11 to 19; and the nations left behind to test Israel, verse 20 through chapter 3 verse 6.

units.label: A generation that did not know the LORD
units.note: the memory of God's works fades

verses.text: After Joshua had dismissed the people, the Israelites went out to take possession of the land, each to his own inheritance.
And the people served the LORD throughout the days of Joshua and of the elders who outlived him, who had seen all the great works that the LORD had done for Israel.
And Joshua son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died at the age of 110.
They buried him in the land of his inheritance, at Timnath-heres in the hill country of Ephraim, north of Mount Gaash.
After that whole generation had also been gathered to their fathers, another generation rose up who did not know the LORD or the works that He had done for Israel.
And the Israelites did evil in the sight of the LORD and served the {{baal|Baals}}.

Thus they forsook the LORD, the God of their fathers, who had brought them out of the land of Egypt, and they followed after various gods of the peoples around them. They bowed down to them and provoked the LORD to anger,
for they forsook Him and served Baal and the Ashtoreths.

Then the anger of the LORD burned against Israel, and He delivered them into the hands of those who plundered them. He sold them into the hands of their enemies all around, whom they were no longer able to resist.
Wherever Israel marched out, the hand of the LORD was against them to bring calamity, just as He had sworn to them. So they were greatly distressed.
Then the LORD raised up {{shophet|judges}}, who {{yasha|saved}} them from the hands of those who plundered them.

Israel, however, did not listen to their judges. Instead, they prostituted themselves with other gods and bowed down to them. They quickly turned from the way of their fathers, who had walked in obedience to the LORD’s commandments; they did not do as their fathers had done.

Whenever the LORD raised up a judge for the Israelites, He was with that judge and saved them from the hands of their enemies while the judge was still alive; for the LORD was moved to pity by their groaning under those who oppressed them and afflicted them.

But when the judge died, the Israelites became even more corrupt than their fathers, going after other gods to serve them and bow down to them. They would not give up their evil practices and stubborn ways.

units.label: Nations left to test Israel
units.note: the LORD proves the heart of His people

verses.text: So the anger of the LORD burned against Israel, and He said, “Because this nation has transgressed the covenant I laid down for their fathers and has not heeded My voice,
I will no longer drive out before them any of the nations Joshua left when he died.
In this way I will {{nasah|test}} whether Israel will keep the way of the LORD by walking in it as their fathers did.”
That is why the LORD had left those nations in place and had not driven them out immediately by delivering them into the hand of Joshua.
These are the nations that the LORD left to {{nasah|test}} all the Israelites who had not known any of the wars in Canaan,
if only to teach warfare to the subsequent generations of Israel, especially to those who had not known it formerly:
the five rulers of the Philistines, all the Canaanites, the Sidonians, and the Hivites who lived in the mountains of Lebanon from Mount Baal-hermon to Lebo-hamath.
These nations were left to test the Israelites, to find out whether they would keep the commandments of the LORD, which He had given their fathers through Moses.
Thus the Israelites continued to live among the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.
And they took the daughters of these people in marriage, gave their own daughters to their sons, and served their gods.

baal.gloss: Lord or master, the name of the chief Canaanite storm and fertility god, worshiped alongside the goddess figures called Ashtoreths or Asherahs. To serve the Baals was to trade the living God who brought Israel out of Egypt for the local gods of the land, thought to control rain, crops, and offspring. This exchange is the root sin of the whole cycle.

shophet.gloss: Judge, ruler, deliverer. The judges are not mainly courtroom figures but Spirit-empowered saviors whom God raises up to rescue and lead Israel in a time with no king. The BSB footnote allows governors or leaders. Each judge brings a real but temporary reprieve; the office itself points beyond these flawed men to the true Judge and King.

yasha.gloss: To save, deliver, rescue. God raises up judges who yasha, save, His people from their oppressors. The word is the root behind the names Joshua and Jesus, and it keeps the focus on God as the true Savior working through the judge; the repeated saving, always undone by relapse, builds a longing for a salvation that lasts.

nasah.gloss: To test, try, prove. The LORD leaves the nations in the land to test whether Israel will keep His way. The testing is not God discovering something He did not know, but exposing and proving the true state of Israel's heart, much as the same word describes God testing His people in the wilderness.

basic.context: This passage is the key that unlocks the whole book of Judges. After Joshua and the elders who had seen God's mighty works die off, a new generation grows up that does not know the LORD. From there a grim pattern turns like a wheel: the people do evil and worship the Baals; God, in anger, hands them over to enemies who plunder them; they groan under the oppression; God, moved with pity, raises up a judge who saves them; but when the judge dies they relapse, becoming even worse than before. To make the point, God announces He will leave the surrounding nations in the land, not now as a punishment only but as a test of whether Israel will keep His way. The big question the passage forces is: what happens to a people who forget what God has done, and who can finally break a cycle they cannot break themselves?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative functioning as a theological overview; the narrator is telling us in advance how to read every judge story that follows. We should notice the repeating stages, sin, servitude, supplication, salvation, and then silence and relapse, and feel the downward spiral, each cycle worse than the last. It is describing what happened, not prescribing that God's people must forever fail; rather it exposes the human heart and builds a longing the book itself cannot satisfy. The test of the nations shows God using even Israel's enemies to reveal and refine the true condition of their hearts.
basic.originalLanguages: The root sin is serving the baal, the Canaanite lord of storm and harvest, in place of the God of the exodus. Into the wreckage God raises up shophet, judges, better called deliverers, who yasha, save, His people, the very verb behind the names Joshua and Jesus. And God leaves the nations to nasah, test, Israel, to prove what is really in their hearts. Serving Baal, saved by a judge, tested by the nations: the words themselves narrate the cycle.
basic.typology: Every judge who saves Israel and then dies, leaving them to relapse, is a small and failing picture of the Savior to come, one who saves not for a generation but forever, and who does not die and leave His people to fall away, but having died, rises and lives to keep them. The endless cycle, always sliding lower, points to the need for a new heart and a King who will not fail, both promised beyond the book and given in Christ.
basic.christ: The saddest word in the passage may be when the judge died, for every deliverance dies with its deliverer and the people sink lower. This is the ache the gospel answers. The judges save through the word yasha, the very root of the name Jesus, the Savior who does not merely rescue for a season. Christ, the true Judge and King, saves His people from a deeper enemy than Moab or Midian, and because He rose and lives forever, His salvation cannot be undone by His death. Where the judges left Israel to relapse, Christ gives His people a new heart and His own Spirit so that the cycle is finally broken.
basic.god: God is holy and cannot be indifferent to idolatry; His anger burns justly against a people who forsake Him. Yet the passage overflows with mercy: again and again He is moved to pity by their groaning and raises up a savior, though they have done nothing to deserve it. He is sovereign even over Israel's enemies, using the nations both to discipline and to test His people's hearts. And He is patient beyond reckoning, refusing to abandon a stubborn people even as they grow worse, until the fuller answer to their need comes in Christ.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 6 warned Israel to teach the next generation the works of the LORD lest they forget. Exodus 34 describes the LORD as compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, the mercy seen in every rescue here. Nehemiah 9 retells this very cycle as a confession of God's patience and Israel's rebellion. And Judges 21 ends the book with the refrain that everyone did what was right in his own eyes, for there was no king.
basic.oneStory: This is the engine room of Judges, the cycle laid bare: a forgetful generation, idolatry, oppression, groaning, and a God who keeps sending deliverers to a people who keep falling away. Each rescue dies with its judge, and each relapse sinks lower, so that the whole book cries out for a King who will not die and a Savior who gives His people a new heart. That cry is answered in Jesus, the true Judge, whose salvation lasts because He lives forever.

context.literary: Judges 2:6 through 3:6 is the programmatic prologue to the body of the book. It briefly revisits Joshua's death, already noted in 1:1, to mark the end of a faithful generation, then lays out the theological cycle in verses 11 to 19 and closes with the theme of the nations left as a test. Everything after this, the accounts of Othniel, Ehud, Deborah, Gideon, Samson, and the rest, is a set of variations on the pattern announced here. The section is built on repetition, the same verbs of doing evil, serving other gods, and being handed over recurring with deliberate force.
context.historical: The setting is the period of the judges, roughly the centuries between Joshua and the rise of the monarchy, when Israel had no central king and lived as scattered tribes among Canaanite peoples. Baal and the Ashtoreths, or Asherahs, were the dominant Canaanite deities, gods of storm, fertility, and harvest, and the pressure to adopt their worship was intense in an agricultural land. Intermarriage with the surrounding peoples, named at the close of the section, was the practical bridge by which their gods entered Israel's homes.
context.canonical: The passage gathers up earlier covenant warnings, from Deuteronomy and Joshua, that forgetting God's works and mingling with the nations would lead to apostasy and discipline. Its cycle of rebellion, judgment, mercy, and relapse is retold as national confession in Nehemiah 9 and Psalm 106. The repeated failure of the judges, and the book's closing refrain that there was no king, set up the longing for a righteous king that runs through Samuel and the Psalms and finds its answer in Christ, the true Judge and King.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give the reader the interpretive pattern for the whole book, showing how a generation's forgetting of the LORD sets off a repeating and worsening cycle of idolatry, oppression, and merciful deliverance, and to expose Israel's need for a lasting salvation that the judges could never provide.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The section describes Israel's recurring apostasy and God's recurring mercy; it does not prescribe the cycle as inevitable for God's people, nor does it commend Israel's relapses. The enduring lessons are the danger of a generation that does not know God's works, the justice and the mercy of God, and the insufficiency of every human deliverer to change the human heart.

debates.question: In what sense did the new generation not know the LORD, in verse 10?

views.label: A failure of transmission
views.case: The previous generation did not adequately pass on the memory of God's mighty works, as Deuteronomy 6 had commanded; the new generation grew up ignorant of what the LORD had done, so the not knowing is largely a catechetical and covenantal breakdown between generations.

views.label: A failure of the heart, not mere information
views.case: To know the LORD in Hebrew thought is relational and covenantal, not just factual; this generation may have had the stories yet did not know God in trust and obedience, so the not knowing is a hardened heart that will not acknowledge Him, which is why it issues at once in serving the Baals.

debates.bearing: Both matter for the church: we are called to teach the next generation God's works diligently, and to pray that such teaching becomes a living, heart-level knowledge of God and not mere secondhand information.

debates.question: Why does God leave the nations to test Israel rather than simply removing them?

views.label: Discipline for covenant-breaking
views.case: Chapter 2 frames the nations remaining as the announced consequence of Israel's disobedience; God stops driving them out because Israel transgressed the covenant, so the nations are first of all a just chastening for sin.

views.label: A proving and training of the heart
views.case: Chapter 3 adds that the nations are left to test whether Israel will keep the LORD's way, and even to teach warfare to a generation that had not known it; the testing exposes and matures faith, using hardship as a means of proving the heart, as God tested Israel in the wilderness.

debates.bearing: Held together, the two show that God's discipline and God's testing are not opposites: the same left-behind nations both punish sin and prove the heart, and God can use even our unremoved enemies to reveal and refine our faith.

study.originalLanguages: The vocabulary of this section tells the whole story of the book in miniature. The root sin is that Israel served the baal, the Canaanite lord of storm, fertility, and harvest, together with the Ashtoreths, exchanging the God who brought them out of Egypt for the gods of the land they were meant to dispossess. In response the LORD's anger burns and He hands them over, until, moved by their groaning, He raises up a shophet, a judge, though the word means far more than a courtroom official; these are Spirit-empowered deliverers who yasha, save, His people. That verb, to save, is the very root behind the names Joshua and Jesus, and it keeps the true Savior in view behind each human rescuer. Then God announces that He will leave the nations in the land to nasah, to test, Israel, to prove whether the heart that says it belongs to Him will actually keep His way. Serving Baal, saved by a judge, tested by the nations: the three words trace the rise and fall that will repeat, ever downward, through the rest of Judges, until a Savior comes whose deliverance the grave cannot end.

typology.type: The judge who saves Israel and then dies
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Savior who does not merely rescue for a generation but, having died, rises and lives forever to keep His people, Hebrews 7:23-25.

typology.type: The word yasha, to save, at the heart of the cycle
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, whose very name means the LORD saves, the true and lasting Deliverer the judges only foreshadow, Matthew 1:21.

typology.type: The endless downward cycle exposing a hard heart
typology.fulfillment: The new heart and indwelling Spirit God promises and gives in Christ, so that His people no longer relapse but are kept, Ezekiel 36:26-27.

study.christ: The most sorrowful phrase in the passage is when the judge died, for every deliverance dies with its deliverer, and the people sink lower than before. This is precisely the ache the gospel answers. The judges rescue by the word yasha, the root of the name Jesus, the Savior who does not save merely for a season. Christ is the true Judge and King who saves His people from an enemy deeper than any Canaanite king, sin and death themselves, and because He rose and lives forever, His salvation can never be undone by His death; He always lives to intercede for those He saves. Where the judges left Israel to relapse the moment they were gone, Christ gives His people a new heart and pours out His own Spirit, so that the cycle of apostasy is at last broken, not by a better generation, but by a Savior who cannot die again.
study.god: This passage holds God's justice and God's mercy in the same frame. He is holy, and His anger burns justly against a people who forsake Him for the Baals; He will not treat idolatry as a small thing. Yet mercy runs through every turn of the wheel: again and again He is moved to pity by their groaning and raises up a savior, though they have earned only judgment. He is sovereign over the nations, wielding even Israel's enemies to discipline and to test His people's hearts, and He is astonishingly patient, refusing to cast off a stubborn people who grow worse rather than better. The God of the cycle is the God who will finally break it, providing in Christ the salvation and the new heart His people could never produce.

crossReferences.note: The command to teach the next generation the LORD's works diligently, lest, once settled and full, they forget Him, the very failure of the generation in Judges 2:10.
The LORD is compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love, the character behind His repeated pity and deliverance in the cycle.
A later confession that retells this exact cycle of rebellion, oppression, crying out, and merciful rescue, magnifying God's patience.
The book's closing refrain, everyone did what was right in his own eyes, for there was no king, naming the deeper need the cycle exposes.

study.oneStory: This section is the engine room of Judges, laying the cycle bare: a forgetful generation, idolatry, oppression, groaning, and a God who keeps sending deliverers to a people who keep falling away. Each rescue dies with its judge, and each relapse sinks lower, so that the whole book cries out for a King who will not die and a Savior who gives His people a new heart. That cry runs on through Israel's history and is answered at last in Jesus, the true Judge and King, whose salvation lasts because He Himself lives forever.

quiz.q: What triggers the whole cycle of apostasy in this passage?

options.text: A new generation arose that did not know the LORD or the works He had done for Israel.
options.note: Verse 10 names the forgetting of God and His works as the root of the descent into idolatry.

options.text: Israel simply grew tired of the hard work of farming.
options.note: The land's pressures were real, but the text points to not knowing the LORD, not weariness, as the cause.

options.text: The judges led the people astray.
options.note: The judges were deliverers God raised up; the relapse came after them, not from them.

options.text: God stopped loving His people.
options.note: The opposite: God repeatedly showed pity and raised up saviors even as the people fell away.

quiz.q: What are the recurring stages of the cycle described here?

options.text: The people sin, are given into servitude, groan to God, are saved by a judge, then fall silent and relapse.
options.note: Verses 11 to 19 lay out sin, servitude, supplication, salvation, and relapse in order.

options.text: The people sin and are immediately and finally destroyed.
options.note: Judgment comes, but it is followed by God's merciful deliverance, not final destruction.

options.text: The people obey, prosper, and are blessed without interruption.
options.note: This ignores the idolatry and oppression that drive the whole cycle.

options.text: The judges conquer all the surrounding nations permanently.
options.note: The nations remain, and each deliverance is temporary, undone when the judge dies.

quiz.q: Why does the LORD leave the surrounding nations in the land?

options.text: Both to discipline Israel for covenant-breaking and to test whether they will keep His way.
options.note: Chapter 2 frames it as consequence and chapter 3 as a test; the two hold together.

options.text: Only because He was unable to drive them out.
options.note: He had driven nations out before; He now leaves them deliberately, as discipline and test, not from inability.

options.text: Because the nations had become faithful to the LORD.
options.note: The nations remained pagan; their presence tested and disciplined Israel, it did not reward their faith.

options.text: Because God had forgotten His promises to Israel.
options.note: God had not forgotten; leaving the nations served His purposes of justice and testing.

quiz.q: How does the office of judge point forward to Christ?

options.text: Each judge saves for a time and then dies, longing for a Savior who saves forever and does not leave His people to relapse.
options.note: The word yasha, to save, is the root of Jesus, who lives forever to keep those He saves.

options.text: The judges prove that people can save themselves if they try hard enough.
options.note: The judges were raised up by God to save; the point is human helplessness, not self-salvation.

options.text: The judges show that God only cares about military victory.
options.note: The deliverances address a deeper spiritual bondage and point beyond battle to salvation of the heart.

options.text: The judges have no connection to Christ.
options.note: Their very role as God-raised saviors, and the word save itself, point straight to Jesus.

quiz.q: What does God's repeated raising up of judges reveal about His character?

options.text: That He is merciful and moved to pity by His people's groaning, even when they deserve only judgment.
options.note: Verse 18 says He was moved to pity by their groaning, the mercy that drives every rescue.

options.text: That He is patient but ultimately indifferent to their idolatry.
options.note: He is patient, but far from indifferent; His anger burns justly against their idolatry.

options.text: That He rescues only those who first reform themselves.
options.note: He rescues an undeserving, unreformed people out of sheer pity, not as a reward for reform.

options.text: That He rescues no one and leaves them to their fate.
options.note: The opposite: again and again He raises up saviors for a people who keep falling away.

questions: A generation arose that did not know the LORD or His works. What are you doing to pass on to those coming after you a living knowledge of what God has done?
The cycle turned on forgetting. Where are you prone to forget God's past faithfulness, and how might remembering it guard you from drifting?
Every deliverance died with its judge. How does it steady you to know that Jesus, unlike any human helper, lives forever to keep the people He saves?
God left the nations in the land to test whether Israel would keep His way. What ongoing difficulty in your life might God be using to prove and refine your heart?
God was moved to pity by His people's groaning even when they deserved judgment. How does His mercy toward an undeserving people give you courage to turn back to Him today?

translationNotes.note: Judges renders the Hebrew shophet; the BSB footnote notes it can mean governors or leaders. These are Spirit-empowered deliverers, not merely legal officials, throughout the book.
The Ashtoreths, and the Asherahs of 3:7, are the female Canaanite deities worshiped alongside Baal; the plural forms point to the many local shrines and images of these fertility gods.
Aram-naharaim, mentioned just beyond this passage, is glossed in the BSB footnote as Mesopotamia, Aram of the two rivers; place names like this locate the oppressors named in the cycle.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 2:6-3:6. No em or en dashes present.
